stem: Evaluate \( \int_{0}^{2\pi} \sin([[m]]x)\sin([[n]]x)\,dx \), where \( [[m]] \) and \( [[n]] \) are distinct positive integers.
solution: Use \( \sin(A)\sin(B) = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)] \). Since \( [[m]] \ne [[n]] \), both \( [[m]]-[[n]] \) and \( [[m]]+[[n]] \) are nonzero integers, so \( \cos \) of each integrates to \( 0 \) over a full period \( [0,2\pi] \); the two sine modes are orthogonal and the integral vanishes: \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the self-overlap (normalization) value \( \pi \), which only applies when \( m=n \).
This doubles the normalization value, mistaking this for a different identity.
This treats the difference of frequencies as if it were the integral's value.
This treats the reciprocal frequency difference as if it were the integral's value.
This has the wrong sign on the normalization value \( \pi \).
This uses half the normalization value \( \pi \).

stem: Evaluate \( \int_{0}^{2\pi} \cos([[m]]x)\cos([[n]]x)\,dx \), where \( [[m]] \) and \( [[n]] \) are distinct positive integers.
solution: Use \( \cos(A)\cos(B) = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)] \). Since \( [[m]] \ne [[n]] \), both \( [[m]]-[[n]] \) and \( [[m]]+[[n]] \) are nonzero integers, so each \( \cos \) term integrates to \( 0 \) over the full period \( [0,2\pi] \); the two cosine modes are orthogonal and the integral vanishes: \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the self-overlap (normalization) value \( \pi \), which only applies when \( m=n \).
This doubles the normalization value, mistaking this for a different identity.
This treats the sum of frequencies as if it were the integral's value.
This treats the reciprocal frequency sum as if it were the integral's value.
This has the wrong sign on the normalization value \( \pi \).
This uses half the normalization value \( \pi \).

stem: Evaluate \( \int_{0}^{2\pi} [[k]]\sin^{2}([[n]]x)\,dx \) (the self-overlap, or normalization, case of the orthogonality relations).
solution: Use \( \sin^{2}(nx) = \tfrac{1-\cos(2nx)}{2} \); the cosine term integrates to \( 0 \) over the full period, leaving \( [[k]]\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 2\pi = [[k]]\pi = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This doubles the correct value, as if forgetting the \( \tfrac{1}{2} \) factor from the half-angle identity.
This halves the correct value.
This mistakenly scales the result by the frequency \( [[n]] \), which cancels out of this integral.
This mistakenly divides by the frequency \( [[n]] \), which cancels out of this integral.
